---
title: "Getting Started with an Agent"
---

Coding agents are powerful tools, but they come with significant risks.  To use
agents safely, it’s important to run them in an environment where the potential
harms are mitigated. Coder Workspaces are very helpful for this purpose because
they are disposable virtual machines. Any agent running in a workspace can only
interact with the files you upload to the VM. This provides more safety than
running the agent on your personal computer, for example. 


## The Coder Workspace Environment

::: {.callout-note}
Coder workspaces are a UCSB-specific resource, however you could substitute
any cloud-based service for running virtual machines.
:::

Follow the [setup instructions](../setup.qmd) if necessary, to create a [Coder Workspace](https://coder.dreamlab.ucsb.edu). You should have a workspace with a dashboard like the image below.

![Coder Workspace Dashboard](../images/coder_dashboard.png){fig-alt="Example Coder Workspace dashboard with buttons to stop and restart the workspace, and open VS Code"}

Start VS Code from the workspace dashboard. The web-based editor starts in an empty directory (`~/workspace`). 

Explore the VS Code interface:

- Create a new file, called `README.md` 
- Open a terminal window

![VS Code Web, with Terminal window for running bash commands](../images/vscode_terminal.png){fig-alt="VS Code Web interface with terminal window show output from `ls` command."}

### Git Setup

We will need to configure git in order to create commits later on.

```bash
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "you@ucsb.edu"

```

## Our Coding Agent: OpenCode

There are many coding agents out there. We like opencode because it was one of
the few widely used coding agents that is model agnostic. 

To start OpenCode, run `opencode` in the VS Code terminal.

![VS Code window with OpenCode running in a terminal](../images/vscode_opencode.png){fig-alt="VS Code Web with OpenCode running."}

By default, OpenCode on Coder Workspaces is configured to use open weights
models through the College IT Gateway.

OpenCode needs to know which AI model to talk to. We'll point it at the **DREAM
Lab AI Gateway**, which gives us access to a range of models through a single API
key.

**1. Connect to a model provider.** In the OpenCode prompt, type `/connect` and
press <kbd>Enter</kbd>. Start typing `dream` to filter the list, then select
**DREAM Lab AI Gateway** under *Providers*.

![Selecting the DREAM Lab AI Gateway provider](../images/opencode_connect_provider.png){fig-alt="OpenCode model selection dropdown with DREAM Lab AI Gateway highlighted"}

**2. Enter your API key.** Paste the API key you were given for the workshop and
press <kbd>Enter</kbd> to submit.

![Entering your API key](../images/opencode_connect_apikey.png){fig-alt="OpenCode prompt entering an API key"}

**3. Select a model.** Choose **Gemini 3.5 Flash** from the list of available
models. You can use (Ctrl+F) to "favorite" a model. We will use both Gemini 3.5
Flash and Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview (Custom Tools) -- you might want to favorite
both of these to make switching models a little easier.

![Selecting the Gemini 3.5 Flash model](../images/opencode_connect_model.png){fig-alt="OpenCode model selection dropdown with Gemini 3.5 Flash highlighted"}

OpenCode is now configured and ready to use the DREAM Lab AI Gateway.

## Our First Prompt

To demonstrate the capabilities of a coding agent, let's ask OpenCode to inspect
that machine we're using.

```md
Tell me about the machine you're running on: OS, resources, etc.
```

OpenCode will probably ask you for permission to run a bash command to
inspect the machine. It's safe to say yes because this isn't your real machine!

The response should look something like this.

```md
I will run a few non-modifying commands to retrieve information 
about the operating system, CPU, memory, and disk resources.

bash tool: "uname -a && cat /etc/os-release && free -h && nproc && df -h /"

This machine is running:
- **OS**: Ubuntu 26.04 LTS (Kernel 7.0.0, x86_64)
- **CPU**: 4 Cores
- **RAM**: 15 GiB (12 GiB available)
- **Disk**: 14 GiB total, 8.1 GiB free (41% used)
```

## Agent Permissions

Most coding agents, including OpenCode, have a **permission system** used to
control what actions the agent is allowed to perform in its environment. In the
example above, OpenCode paused and asked before running a bash command. Even
though our agent is isolated in a throwaway virtual machine, it's worth
understanding how it decides when to ask for your approval.

The actions the agent can perform (e.g., reading files, editing them, running shell
commands, fetching URLs) are determined by permission levels:

- **`allow`** — the action runs automatically, no questions asked.
- **`ask`** — the agent pauses and waits for your approval.
- **`deny`** — the action is blocked entirely.

By default **OpenCode allows most actions**, but you can change this through
OpenCode's configuration file.

A few defaults worth knowing:

- Reading files outside the working directory defaults to `ask`.
- `.env` files are denied by default, so secrets aren't read accidentally.

## OpenCode Commands

You can type "`/`" followed by a command name to configure OpenCode or perform various actions. You can find a complete list of commands in the [OpenCode documentation](https://opencode.ai/docs/tui/#commands). 

The commands we will use in the workshop are listed here:

- `/connect` - Add a model provider. Allows you to select from available providers and add their API keys.
- `/exit` - Exit OpenCode. (You can also use `/q`)
- `/help` - Display help information 
- `/models` - List and switch between models
- `/new` - Start a new session (fresh context)


## Exercise: Download and Plot NOAA Tide Data

:::{.callout-note}
Complete the exercise individually but discuss results in small groups (2-5 people).
:::

Use `/new` to start a new session (if necessary) and `/model` to select the
`Gemini 3.1 Pro (Custom Tools)` model. Then cut and paste the following prompt
into OpenCode:

> Create a new folder `noaa-tides` and use it as the working directory; then
> download monthly mean high water for all NOAA stations in California over the
> last ten years. Download as CSV and save in `noaa-tides/data`.

Wait for the agent to complete the task (it may take a few minutes), then answer
the following questions:

- What files were downloaded to `noaa-tides/data`? How many and what format? If
  they are CSVs (as expected), what columns do they include?
- Does everyone in the group have the same set of files? What's the same, and
  what's different?
- Look at the Agent's session transcript: how did the agent complete the task?
  (If it used a language or technique you're not familiar with, **ask it to
  explain the steps it took*)
- In your group, discuss the different approaches the agent took.

As a group, discuss results from the following prompts:

> "what is the name of the station with the highest average mean high water
> value over the last decade?"

> "plot the mean high water value for all stations using a different color for
> each station. Include labels on x/y axes and a legend with the station name"

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
### Discussion Notes

These notes summarize one representative run (Gemini 3.1 Pro, Custom Tools).
Your own session will differ in the details — that variability is part of the
point.

**What files were downloaded?**

18 CSV files, one per California tide station, saved to `noaa-tides/data` and
named by station ID (e.g. `9410170_monthly_mean.csv`). Each file is the NOAA
`monthly_mean` product, so even though we only asked for mean high water, every
CSV contains *all* the monthly datum columns:

```
Year, Month, Highest, MHHW, MHW, MSL, MTL, MLW, MLLW, DTL, GT, MN, DHQ, DLQ, HWI, LWI, Lowest, Inferred
```
Each file holds ~120 monthly rows (June 2016 – May 2026), and some cells (e.g.
`HWI`/`LWI`) are blank where NOAA had no value.

**How did the agent complete the task? (transcript walkthrough)**

1. Probed the NOAA metadata API
   (`mdapi/.../stations.json?state=CA`) and the data API
   (`datagetter?...product=monthly_mean`) with small one-off commands to learn
   the response shape *before* writing any real code.
2. Wrote a `download.py` that looped over stations, requested CSV, and slept
   between calls.
3. Hit two snags and debugged them live:
   - The `?state=CA` filter on the stations endpoint was **silently ignored** —
     the first run started downloading *every* US station (Hawaii, the East
     Coast, the Gulf) instead of just California. The agent caught this, then
     fetched the full station list and filtered **client-side** on
     `state == 'CA'`, which correctly yielded 18 stations.
   - The API began returning `403 Forbidden` after rapid-fire requests (rate
     limiting). The agent diagnosed it with `curl -i`, then added a browser-like
     `User-Agent` header and increased the delay to 1 second per request.
4. Re-ran cleanly, then read one CSV back to confirm the format before
   reporting done.

The agent didn't get it right on the first try. When it encountered errors, it
diagnosed and worked around them.

One choice the agent made *silently*: it requested the data with `datum=MHW`,
so every `MHW` value is measured relative to each station's own MHW datum rather
than an absolute reference. The task never specified a datum, the agent never
mentioned picking one, and — as the follow-up question below shows — that
unstated choice changes the answer. 

**Follow-up prompt: highest average MHW station**

The agent reported **North Spit** as station with the highest average MHW. 

![Plot from NOAA Tides exercise](../images/exercise-noaa-tides.png){fig-alt="Plot of downloaded station MHW values"}

The agent averaged the `MHW` column across each file and reported **North Spit**
(≈0.154 m). That arithmetic is correct *for the data it downloaded* — but it's
probably not the best answer to the question.

The download requested `datum=MHW`, so every `MHW` value in the CSVs is measured
*relative to each station's own long-term MHW datum*. That makes the numbers
tiny (≈0.05–0.15 m) because they aren't tide heights at all — they're
**anomalies**, i.e. how far each month sat above that station's historical
baseline. So "North Spit is highest" really means *its* high water has risen the
most relative to *its own* reference (a local sea-level/subsidence signal), not
that it has the highest tides.

Ask for the same thing against a fixed physical datum (`datum=MLLW`, the actual
height of mean high water above the chart datum) and the answer changes:
**Redwood City** is highest at ≈2.36 m, with North Spit second (≈2.03 m).
Redwood City sits deep in San Francisco Bay, where the tidal range is amplified,
so that result makes physical sense.

**Fixing the prompt**

The root problem is that the original prompt left the datum unspecified, so the
agent picked one for us. We can remove that ambiguity by naming the datum (and
the units) directly. A better version of the download prompt:

> Create a new folder `noaa-tides` and use it as the working directory. Download
> the NOAA monthly mean high water (MHW) for every tide station in California
> over the last ten years, as CSV, into `noaa-tides/data`. Report the heights
> **relative to the MLLW datum, in meters**, so values are comparable across
> stations. Before downloading everything, show me the URL and the columns for
> one station and confirm the datum with me.

The improvements:

- **Names the datum and units** (`MLLW`, meters) so "highest MHW" means actual
  tide height, not an anomaly — and is comparable between stations.
- **Asks the agent to confirm before bulk-downloading**, surfacing hidden
  choices (like the datum) *before* they silently shape the result.

This is the general lesson: when a result depends on an assumption, push that
assumption into the prompt rather than letting the agent fill it in.

:::
